g.data-narration: Welcome to Normal Forms. Learn to design clean databases by organizing tables to reduce redundancy and prevent anomalies.
g.text: Normal Forms Based on Primary Keys

g.data-narration: Normalization organizes database tables to reduce redundancy, prevent data anomalies, and improve integrity through decomposition.
g.text: The process of organizing data in a database to reduce
redundancy and improve data integrity through table
decomposition. Introduced by Edgar F. Codd in 1970s.

g.data-narration: Functional dependencies constrain attribute relationships. Candidate keys uniquely identify rows, while prime attributes appear in keys.
1NF requires atomic values only - no repeating groups or lists in any cell. Each attribute must hold single indivisible values.
g.text: A relation is in 1NF if it contains only atomic (indivisible)
values. No repeating groups or arrays within columns.

g.data-narration: See the transformation: multi-valued courses column expands into separate rows, making StudentID+Course the composite key.
2NF builds on 1NF by eliminating partial dependencies - when non-key attributes depend on only part of a composite key.
g.text: A relation is in 2NF if in 1NF AND no non-prime attribute is
partially dependent on any proper subset of a candidate key.
(e.g., A → C where AB is key)

g.data-narration: In Enroll relation with composite key (StudentID, CourseID), StudentName depends only on StudentID - a partial dependency.
g.text: 2NF Example: Enroll(StudentID, CourseID, StudentName, Grade, Fee)
Given: StudentID → StudentName (partial dependency - StudentName depends on

g.data-narration: 3NF eliminates transitive dependencies where non-key attributes depend on other non-key attributes, not directly on the key.
g.text: A relation is in 3NF if in 2NF AND no transitive dependency
exists (non-key attribute determining another non-key attribute).

g.data-narration: Given A→B, B→C, C→D, the key A transitively determines C and D. Decompose into separate relations for each FD.
g.text: 3NF Example: R(A,B,C,D) with FDs A→B, B→C, C→D
Candidate key: A (since A→B→C→D gives all attributes)
Transitive: A→B→C and B→C→D (non-key determines non-key)

g.data-narration: BCNF is stricter than 3NF. For every FD X→Y, X must be a superkey. It handles overlapping candidate keys properly.
g.text: A relation is in BCNF if for every non-trivial FD X→Y, X must
be a superkey. Stricter than 3NF, handles overlapping candidate keys.

g.data-narration: With FDs A→B and B→C, check: A→B is in BCNF (A is key), but B→C violates BCNF (B is not a superkey).
g.text: BCNF Example: R(A,B,C) with FDs A→B, B→C
Check: A→B (A is key ✓), B→C (B not superkey ✗)

g.data-narration: Normal forms build hierarchically: BCNF implies 3NF implies 2NF implies 1NF. Each level eliminates specific dependency problems.
g.text: Each level eliminates more dependency problems

g.data-narration: Candidate keys are minimal superkeys. Primary key is the selected candidate key. Prime attributes appear in any candidate key.
For exams: identify keys first, then check each normal form sequentially. Practice decomposition problems thoroughly.
Remember: 1NF eliminates repeating groups, 2NF removes partial dependencies, 3NF removes transitive dependencies, BCNF strengthens key requirements.
